content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 6, 2020

Daniel J. McHugh
City Attorney
P O Box 3005
Redlands, CA 92373

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-19-231

Dear Mr. McHugh:

     This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Redlands employee Chris
Boatman regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and
Government Code section 1090, et seq.¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the
Act and Section 1090, not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law
conflict of interest. We also do not address any restrictions on incompatible activities the City may
impose, which are also outside the purview of the Act.

     In addition, we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC
Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the
case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional
advice.

     We are required to forward your request regarding Section 1090 and all pertinent facts
relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Bernardino County District
Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written
response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for
purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against
any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(e)(5).)

                                    QUESTION

     Would the Act or Section 1090 prohibit Mr. Boatman from participating in decisions
regarding two City lease agreements with a nonprofit organization if he were to become a member
of the nonprofit's board of directors?

_____________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-231
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

No. As explained below, neither the Act nor Section 1090 would prohibit Mr. Boatman from
participation in decisions regarding the City's two lease agreements with the nonprofit if he were to
become a member of its board.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are the City Attorney for the City of Redlands seeking this advice on behalf of Chris
Boatman, the Director of the City's Facilities and Community Services Department ("Facilities
Department"). The Facilities Department, formerly the Quality of Life Department, ensures
Redlands residents, businesses and visitors enjoy City services and facilities including parks and
recreation. The Recreation Division of the Facilities Department, whose mission is to provide fun,
safe and informative programs to the community, maintains quality recreation facilities and
services, and facilitates leisure opportunities to encourage healthy lifestyles choices that foster
healthy living.²

Mr. Boatman has received a request from the Redlands Bowl Performing Arts Inc.
("RBPA"), a 501(c)(3) nonprofit corporation, to become a member of its Board of Directors.
Members of the Board serve a term of three years and may serve up to two consecutive terms.
Board members do not receive any compensation from the RBPA for the services they perform.

According to its website, "[f]or almost a century, Redlands Bowl Performing Arts (RBPA),
has served the city of Redlands and the Inland Empire region by providing quality performing arts
to the community free of admission charge via their core program, the Redlands Bowl Summer
Music Festival. Started in 1923 by Grace Stewart Mullen, the RBPA has brought professional
quality, classically-oriented performing arts to the community for over 90 years, and the Redlands
Bowl Summer Music Festival holds the distinction of being the oldest continuously running
summer music festival in the United States where no admission is charged. In addition, the RBPA
has numerous programs to address the needs of under-served youth in the region.³

Mr. Boatman has no direct financial interest related to the RBPA or the properties owned or
leased by the RBPA. In addition, he does not have any financial interest in properties located near
properties owned or leased by the RBPA. However, the City has entered into two agreements with
the RBPA pursuant to which the RBPA leases City-owned facilities. Mr. Boatman's Department is
principally responsible for overseeing the terms and conditions of those agreements, and Mr.
Boatman is involved in City and Department decisions relating to the RBPA.

² (See https://www.cityofredlands.org/node/1521.)

³ (See https://redlandsbowl.org/about-us.)

File No. A-19-231
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

The Act

As Director of the Facilities Department, Mr. Boatman is subject to the Act's conflict of
interest provisions. Under Section 87100, a public official may not make, participate in making, or
use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on one or more of the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).)
Section 87103 identifies interests from which a conflict of interest may arise and includes:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)).

• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000
or more. (Section 87103(b).)

• An interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e).)

• An interest in a source of gifts to the official if the gifts aggregate to $500 or more within
12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e).)

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate
family, also known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.)

For purposes of the Act, a nonprofit organization such as the RBPA is not considered a
"business entity." (Section 82005.) Moreover, as an uncompensated officer of the RBPA, it does
not appear that Mr. Boatman would have an interest in the organization as a source of income.
Assuming he had no other interests implied by the decisions, Mr. Boatman does not have a
potentially disqualifying interest in decisions regarding the City's two lease agreements with RBPA
under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act.

Section 1090

Section 1090 codifies the common law prohibition against "self-dealing" with respect to
governmental contracts. Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their
official capacities, from making contracts in which they are financially interested. The prohibition
applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson
v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.)

Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests,
that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering
the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Employees have been
found to have a financial interest in a contract that involves their employer, even where the contract

File No. A-19-231
Page No. 4

would not result in a change in income or directly involve the employee because an employee has
an overall interest in the financial success of the firm and continued employment.

The Legislature, however, has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's
prohibition where the interest involved is deemed a "remote interest," as defined in Section 1091 or
a "noninterest," as defined in Section 1091.5. If a noninterest is present, the public official's
abstention is generally not required, and the contract may be made by the agency.

The noninterest exception in Section 1091.5(a)(8) provides an officer or employee is not
deemed interested in a contract if his or her interest is:

That of a noncompensated officer of a nonprofit, tax-exempt
corporation, which, as one of its primary purposes, supports the
functions of the body or board or to which the body or board has a legal
obligation to give particular consideration, and provided further that this
interest is noted in its official records.

For purposes of this paragraph, an officer is "noncompensated" even
though he or she receives reimbursement from the nonprofit, tax-exempt
corporation for necessary travel and other actual expenses incurred in
performing the duties of his or her office.

You stated that RBPA is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization, and Mr. Boatman would
receive no compensation by serving on its board. Additionally, based upon the description of the
services it provides, the primary purpose of providing free quality performing arts to the community
while addressing the needs of under-served youth supports an important function of the City,
including the Facilities Department which has a mission to provide fun, safe and informative
programs to the community. Accordingly, if Mr. Boatman notes his interest in the City's official
records, he may participate in contractual decisions involving RBPA.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Jack Woodside
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

JW:aja

sections.question:
Would the Act or Section 1090 prohibit Mr. Boatman from participating in decisions . 
regarding two City lease agreements with a nonprofit organization if he were to become a member 
of the nonprofit' s board of directors?
sections.conclusion:
No. As explained below, neither the Act nor Section 1090 would prohibit Mr. Boatman from 
participation in decisions regarding the City's two lease agreements with the nonprofit if he were to 
become a member of its board. 
·

regarding two City lease agreements with a nonprofit organization if he were to become a member 
of the nonprofit' s board of directors?

CONCLUSION: No. As explained below, neither the Act nor Section 1090 would prohibit Mr. Boatman from 
participation in decisions regarding the City's two lease agreements with the nonprofit if he were to 
become a member of its board.
